findings.description: The PDF presents the entire table in a compact, single-page horizontal layout with all columns visible in one row. The HTML conversion breaks the table into a much taller vertical layout with significant whitespace and wrapped cell content, fundamentally changing the compact tabular presentation.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows a dense table with ~30 rows fitting on one page. HTML page 1 shows the same data but with much taller rows due to text wrapping in Owner-Facility and other columns, resulting in content spreading across multiple pages.

findings.description: Table structure is preserved with correct columns and rows, but cell content wrapping differs significantly from the PDF, making rows much taller in the HTML version.
findings.evidence: PDF has compact single-line or two-line entries per row. HTML wraps Owner-Facility names across 3-7 lines per cell, dramatically increasing row heights.

findings.description: Currency formatting and numeric alignment appear preserved, but the overall text density and visual compactness is lost in the HTML version.
findings.evidence: PDF shows tightly formatted monetary values right-aligned in columns. HTML maintains the values but with different visual density.

findings.description: Page 2 table continues with same structure issues as page 1 - correct data but different row heights due to text wrapping.
findings.evidence: PDF page 2 shows remaining ~10 rows plus 'Total Cases: 40' in compact format. HTML spreads this across more vertical space.

findings.description: Reading order is maintained - all rows appear in the same sequence in both PDF and HTML versions.
findings.evidence: Both versions start with LV-2013-0007 and end with SS-2012-0002, with Total Cases: 40 at the bottom.

findings.description: No h1 heading exists in the HTML. The document title 'DWQ January 2013 Civil Penalty Assessments' appears only in a div with class 'page-header' rather than as an h1 heading.
findings.evidence:
The HTML uses
DWQ January 2013 Civil Penalty Assessments
but no h1 tag is present.

findings.description: The HTML has a nested <main> tag inside another <main> tag, but this is a structural issue, not content duplication.
findings.evidence: HTML has <main> wrapping the header and another <main> wrapping the table.
